=== SCENE 1 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

When people imagine the modern school shooter, they usually think of the postc coline archetype.
Perpetrators who are isolated and misanthropic while obsessed with notoriety.

=== SCENE 2 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

Eric Harris and Dan Clelebold became the reference point for how many later cases were understood. [NAME: Eric Harris — later school shooting reference point] [NAME: Dan Clelebold — later school shooting reference point] [SOURCE: newspaper archive]
But one of the most disturbing school shooting cases in American history happened before Coline ever took place.

=== SCENE 3 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

In 1998 in Springfield, Oregon, 15-year-old Kiplan Kingl murdered his parents before opening fire at Thirsten High School. [DATE: 1998] [MAP: Springfield, Oregon] [NAME: Kiplan Kingl — 15-year-old attacker] [SOURCE: court records]
Unlike later attackers who seemed to chase infamy or imitate previous killers, King's case emerged from a collapsing home life, untreated psychosis, and a teenage mind spiraling into paranoia. [SOURCE: psychological evaluations]
To understand how a seemingly ordinary community became the site of one of the late 1990s most haunting tragedies, I've compiled a full research iceberg that follows the case from the attack itself to the lesserknown details surrounding it, even with potential insight as to what caused the massive attack that preceded it.
This is a Kipkinko iceberg. [TEXT: KIPKINKO ICEBERG] [CHAPTER: Before The Attack]

=== SCENE 4 ===
MOOD: tension
PACING: dense

215 1998. [DATE: 1998]
On May 21st, 1998, 15-year-old Kipland Kip King walked into the cafeteria of Thirstston High School in Springfield, Oregon, armed with a semi-automatic rifle. [DATE: May 21st, 1998] [STAT: 15-year-old — attacker age] [MAP: Thirstston High School, Springfield, Oregon] [SOURCE: police report] [EVIDENCE: semi-automatic rifle]

=== SCENE 5 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: normal

He opened fire on his classmates, killing two students, Ben Walker and Macau Nickson, and wounding 25 others. [STAT: two students killed — casualties] [NAME: Ben Walker — student victim] [NAME: Macau Nickson — student victim] [STAT: 25 others — wounded] [SOURCE: police report] [TEXT: TWO DEAD, 25 WOUNDED]
By the time police began piecing together what had happened, they would discover the attackers started the day before when King murdered both of his parents at the family home. [SOURCE: police investigation] [REVEAL] [SILENCE]

=== SCENE 6 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

his parents.
The thirst and shooting did not begin at the school.
It began inside the Kinkle family home. [MAP: Kinkle family home] [REVEAL]

=== SCENE 7 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: normal

After Kif was suspended for bringing a stolen gun to school, he returned home and shot his father, Bill. [NAME: Bill — father] [SOURCE: police report]
Later that evening, when his mother, Faith, came home, he killed her, too. [NAME: Faith — mother] [SOURCE: police report]
Their bodies remained in the house overnight while King prepared for what he was going to do the next morning. [SOURCE: police report] [CENSOR: bodies] [HOLD]

=== SCENE 8 ===
MOOD: tension
PACING: normal

Suspension/expulsion.
The immediate trigger for the thirst and shooting came the day before the attack when King brought a stolen handgun to school. [SOURCE: school disciplinary records] [EVIDENCE: stolen handgun]
He had paid another student $110 for the weapon, but the gun was discovered and Kinko was suspended. [STAT: $110 — weapon payment] [SOURCE: police report]

=== SCENE 9 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

That suspension set off the final collapse.
After being sent home, he took the lives of both his parents, then returned to Thirsten High School the next morning armed for the attack. [MAP: Thirsten High School] [SOURCE: police report]

=== SCENE 10 ===
MOOD: action
PACING: dense

Jacob Reicher. [NAME: Jacob Reicher — wounded student]
As King's rifle ran empty and he began to reload, wounded student Jacob Reiker tackled him. [SOURCE: witness statements]
His brother Dustin Riker along with Adam Wahberger, Doug Yor, and other students rushed to help pin Kinkle to the cafeteria floor. [NAME: Dustin Riker — student helper] [NAME: Adam Wahberger — student helper] [NAME: Doug Yor — student helper] [SOURCE: witness statements]

=== SCENE 11 ===
MOOD: action
PACING: dense

During the struggle, Kingle pulled a Glock from his belt and fired one shot before being disarmed, wounding Riker again and injuring another student. [SOURCE: witness statements] [EVIDENCE: Glock]
As the students restrained him, Kingle shouted, "Just kill me." They held him down until police arrived and arrested him. [QUOTE: Just kill me.] [SOURCE: witness statements] [SILENCE] [TEXT: JUST KILL ME] [HOLD]

=== SCENE 12 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: normal

His sister, Kip King's older sister, Kristen Kinkl, was six years older than him and was studying at Hawaii Pacific University at the time of the Thirsten High School shooting. [NAME: Kristen Kinkl — older sister] [STAT: six years older — age gap] [MAP: Hawaii Pacific University] [SOURCE: family letter]
After the attack, she wrote a letter to Judge Jack Madison, giving one of the most personal accounts of Kip's life before the murders. [NAME: Judge Jack Madison — judge] [SOURCE: court letter]
In the letter, Kristen described Kip as compassionate, sensitive, and eager to please the people around him. [SOURCE: family letter]

=== SCENE 13 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: hold

She said that only with hindsight could she recognize the signs that he had needed a kind of help his family did not know how to give. [SOURCE: family letter]
Her letter does not deny the horror of what he did, but it presents him as someone whose mental collapse had been hidden beneath the surface of what seemed like a normal family life. [SOURCE: family letter]
She also described visiting him after the attack, saying that for weeks he could barely make eye contact. [SOURCE: family letter]
When he finally spoke, he told her, "I am so sorry." According to Kristen, Kip wanted to listen to the victim's families during the hearings because he believed he owed it to them. [QUOTE: I am so sorry.] [SOURCE: family letter]

=== SCENE 14 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: hold

This entry is important because Kristen's letter gives the case a rare perspective from inside the family. [SOURCE: family letter]
She was not only grieving the murder of her parents, she was also trying to understand how the little brother she remembered had become capable of killing them. [SOURCE: family letter]

=== SCENE 15 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: dense

His sentence Kipkle was sentenced to 111 years and 8 months of the possibility of parole. [STAT: 111 years and 8 months — sentence length] [SOURCE: court records] [TEXT: 111 YEARS, 8 MONTHS]
The number came from the structure of the sentence.
25 years for the four murder convictions which would be served at the same time followed by consecutive sentences for the 26 attempted murder counts. [STAT: 25 years — murder sentence] [STAT: four murder convictions — convictions] [STAT: 26 attempted murder counts — counts] [SOURCE: court records]
In practice, this meant King would spend the rest of his life in prison with his earliest release date placed more than a century after the shooting, January 21st, 2110. [DATE: January 21st, 2110] [SOURCE: court records] [HOLD]

=== SCENE 16 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

Paranoid schizophrenia diagnosis. [TEXT: PARANOID SCHIZOPHRENIA]
After the attack, Kingle was looked at by multiple mental health experts. [SOURCE: mental health evaluations]
Court documents state that the experts genuinely agreed he suffered from psychosis and some form of paranoid schizophrenia or a schizopeeffective disorder combining features of schizophrenia and depression. [SOURCE: court documents]

=== SCENE 17 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: hold

King reported that he had been hearing voices since he was around 12 years old, including one voice that encouraged violence. [STAT: 12 years old — reported onset] [SOURCE: court records]
According to court records, he said the voice had instructed him to commit the murders and the school shooting and that he felt he had no choice but to obey it. [SOURCE: court records] [QUOTE: he felt he had no choice but to obey it] [HOLD]

=== SCENE 18 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

Following the recommendation of a child psychologist, Kinko began taking antiscychotic medicine. [SOURCE: child psychologist recommendation]
In July 1999, his attorneys instructed him to stop taking the medication so experts could assess his mental condition more accurately. [DATE: July 1999] [SOURCE: court records]
Court records say his hallucinations and depression worsened after the withdrawal, partly because of the stress of the approaching trial. [SOURCE: court records]

=== SCENE 19 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

This diagnosis became central to the legal and moral debate around the case.
King eventually entered a plea agreement in which the aggravated murder and attempted aggravated murder charges were dismissed while he pleaded guilty to murder and attempted murder. [SOURCE: plea agreement]
The result was a sentence that treated him as legally responsible while still leaving the question of his mental illness at the center of the case. [SOURCE: court records]

=== SCENE 20 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

Confession. [TEXT: RECORDED CONFESSION]
One of the most significant pieces of media connected to the case is King's recorded confession. [SOURCE: police interview archive] [EVIDENCE: recorded confession]
It was taped by detective Al Waran on the day of the Thirstston High School shooting. [NAME: Al Waran — detective] [SOURCE: police interview archive]
Shortly after, Kingle had been taken into custody. [SOURCE: police report]

=== SCENE 21 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: hold

The confession gives limited factual detail about the attack, but it offers a disturbing look at Kingle's mental state in the immediate aftermath. [SOURCE: police interview archive]
Throughout the recording, he repeatedly expresses suicidal thoughts and appears overwhelmed by what he has done. [SOURCE: police interview archive]

=== SCENE 22 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: hold

> > Like, what what was he saying about you? [QUOTE: Like, what what was he saying about you?] [SOURCE: confession tape]
> > [clears throat] [laughter] >> The tape captures the true collapse of his mind. [SOURCE: confession tape]
> > The recording ends abruptly when Kingo asks for water, leaving behind one of the most unsettling documents in the case, the 111/12year sentence confusion. [SOURCE: police interview archive] [HOLD]

=== SCENE 23 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

A smaller point of confusion around Kinko's sentence is a difference between 111 years and 112 years. [STAT: 111 years vs 112 years — sentence confusion] [SOURCE: court records]
The exact sentence was 111 years and 8 months, but many sources round that figure up and describe it as a 112 years sentence. [STAT: 111 years and 8 months — exact sentence] [STAT: 112 years — rounded sentence] [SOURCE: court records]
This is why both numbers appear in discussions of the case, even though they refer to the same prison term. [SOURCE: court records]

=== SCENE 24 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

Varying psychological opinions. [TEXT: PSYCHOLOGICAL OPINIONS]
A major part of Kinko's later legal debate came from the different psychological opinions given about his condition. [SOURCE: appellate court records]
Experts generally agreed that he had serious mental health problems, but they differed on what that meant for his future. [SOURCE: expert testimony]

=== SCENE 25 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: normal

Pediatric neurologist Richard Conor testified that Kinkle showed abnormalities in language and sensory processing. [NAME: Richard Conor — pediatric neurologist] [SOURCE: expert testimony]
He believed these problems suggested damage in parts of the brain linked to overall impulse control, making Kingle more vulnerable to psychotic episodes. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Conor also said that because King was still a juvenile, his prognosis could be hopeful of proper treatment and medication. [SOURCE: expert testimony]

=== SCENE 26 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: dense

Other experts were more cautious.
Child psychologist Orin Bolstad said Kinko's illness was treatable but not fully curable and that his hallucinations improved with antiscychotic medication. [NAME: Orin Bolstad — child psychologist] [SOURCE: expert testimony]
However, he also said he would not want to see Kinkle released without medication and intensive support. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Psychiatrist William Sack gave a similar view saying Kingle could potentially be managed after decades of treatment but would remain dangerous if his illness was not properly controlled. [NAME: William Sack — psychiatrist] [SOURCE: expert testimony]

=== SCENE 27 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: hold

This shows why the Kingle case became so legally complicated.
It was not just a question of whether he was mentally ill, but whether a teenage offender with severe psychosis could ever be safely rehabilitated after committing such extreme violence. [SOURCE: legal analysis]

=== SCENE 28 ===
MOOD: tension
PACING: normal

Fixation on guns and explosives. [TEXT: GUNS AND EXPLOSIVES] [CHAPTER: Warning Signs]
King's obsession with guns and explosives appeared long before the thirst and shooting. [SOURCE: police investigation]
It was discovered that he listed surfing the web for info on how to create explosives as his occupation on an internet service account he'd created. [SOURCE: internet account records]
In middle school, classmates even voted him most likely to start World War II, a joke that did not age well in the slightest. [SOURCE: classmate accounts]

=== SCENE 29 ===
MOOD: tension
PACING: normal

Several students later described how often Kingle talked about weapons. [SOURCE: student interviews]
Jesse Cannon, a 15-year-old thirsten student who rode the bus with him, said King once spoke about bringing guns to school, taking a few lives, and then saving one bullet for himself. [NAME: Jesse Cannon — Thirsten student] [STAT: 15-year-old — student age] [SOURCE: student interview]
Cannon said that the conversation felt so unreal at the time, like they were playing a video game. [SOURCE: student interview]

=== SCENE 30 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

Other classmates recalled seeing Kingle with guns outside school, shooting at birds and animals along rural roads. [SOURCE: classmate accounts]
13-year-old TJ Hardy claimed King once showed him a pipe bomb and said he was going to blow something up. [NAME: TJ Hardy — student witness] [STAT: 13-year-old — witness age] [SOURCE: witness statement] [EVIDENCE: pipe bomb]
Others remembered him bragging about killing animals or describing hunting in unusually graphic terms. [SOURCE: classmate accounts]

=== SCENE 31 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: dense

After the attack, police searching the King home discovered more explosive paraphernalia. [SOURCE: police search records] [EVIDENCE: explosive paraphernalia]
Officers found explosive devices inside the house and called in bomb disposable experts. [SOURCE: police search records] [EVIDENCE: explosive devices]
One device was disarmed while another was reportedly so large that part of the house had to be cut away so it could be removed safely. [SOURCE: police search records]
Investigators also found booby traps, bomb making materials, related literature, a hand grenade, and a case of Howard's shell canisters. [SOURCE: police search records] [EVIDENCE: booby traps] [EVIDENCE: bomb making materials] [EVIDENCE: hand grenade]

=== SCENE 32 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: hold

The discovery showed that the violence at thirst in high school was not the only danger King had created. [SOURCE: police investigation]
Behind the school shooting was a much broader fixation on weapons and destruction. [SOURCE: police investigation]
This entry matters because King's fixation on violence was not invisible to the people around him.
It often came disguised as jokes or just general teen edgginess.
Only after the attack did these jokes begin to look like warnings.

=== SCENE 33 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

Walk through. [TEXT: POLICE WALKTHROUGH]
Another significant piece of media from the case is the police walkthrough filmed on the day of the attack. [SOURCE: police walkthrough footage] [EVIDENCE: walkthrough video]
Detective Al Wan took Kingle back through Thirstston High School and had him retrace his movements. [NAME: Al Wan — detective] [MAP: Thirstston High School] [SOURCE: police walkthrough footage]
During the walkthrough, Kinko explained the attack as investigators led him through the school. [SOURCE: police walkthrough transcript]

=== SCENE 34 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: hold

The transcript shows King giving mostly short, flat answers. [SOURCE: police walkthrough transcript]
He does not offer a detailed explanation for the attack.
When asked why he did it, his response is essentially that he felt he had no other choice. [SOURCE: police walkthrough transcript]

> > Why did you do this? [QUOTE: Why did you do this?] [SOURCE: police walkthrough transcript]
> > Had no other choice. [QUOTE: Had no other choice.] [SOURCE: police walkthrough transcript] [TEXT: NO OTHER CHOICE] [HOLD]
> > He also denied that any specific student had upset him or that anyone had threatened him into carrying out the shooting. [SOURCE: police walkthrough transcript]

=== SCENE 35 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: hold

What makes this walkthrough unsettling is how clinical it is.
Investigators move through the aftermath of the tragedy while King quietly confirms parts of what happened. [SOURCE: police walkthrough footage]
He was acting extremely childlike, as if he was being corrected for ordinary misbehavior rather than someone who had just committed a massacre. [SOURCE: police walkthrough footage]

=== SCENE 36 ===
MOOD: nostalgic
PACING: normal

Early learning difficulties and IQ. [TEXT: LEARNING DIFFICULTIES]
Kingo's early life was shaped by a sharp contrast between family expectations and his own learning difficulties. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
Both of his parents were respected local teachers and people close to the family later described the King household as one where achievement was quietly expected. [SOURCE: family and community accounts]
Kip reportedly told a psychiatrist he felt he had to be 100% and that failing to do his best made him feel like an embarrassment to his parents. [STAT: 100% — personal expectation] [SOURCE: psychiatrist notes]

=== SCENE 37 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: normal

From early childhood, Kinkle struggled in ways that did not fit the image of a successful teacher's son.
He had difficulty learning to tie his shoes, struggled badly with reading, writing, and spelling, and repeated first grade because of concerns about maturity and development. [SOURCE: school records]
His sister Kristen later remembered him studying for spelling tests for hours and still failing, leaving him confused and ashamed because he did not understand what was wrong. [SOURCE: family letter]

=== SCENE 38 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

By third grade, Kinko was diagnosed with a learning disorder and his parents also suspected attention deficit issues. [SOURCE: school records]
In middle school, he told friends he was taking ritland to control his temper. [SOURCE: friend accounts]

=== SCENE 39 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: normal

In the fourth grade, Kip continued to qualify for special education services and was diagnosed with a learning disability. [SOURCE: school records]
He worked with a special education counselor for the year. [SOURCE: school records]
However, according to his fourth grade teacher, he was simultaneously placed in a talented and gifted program because of his above average performance in science and maths. [SOURCE: fourth grade teacher account]

=== SCENE 40 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: normal

At the same time, later psychological testimony made clear that his problems were not a simple lack of intelligence. [SOURCE: psychological testimony]
Psychiatrist William Sack described him as cognitively bright and said his overall IQ was high despite his learning disability. [SOURCE: psychiatric testimony]
This is an interesting cognitive profile which complicates the image of Kingle adding nuance to the typical simply disturbed or simply unintelligent labels. [SOURCE: psychological analysis]

=== SCENE 41 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: dense

Kinkle versus person. [TEXT: KINKLE VERSUS PERSON]
Kinkle versus person was one of the major legal battles over Kip King Kinkle's sentence after the Supreme Court restricted life without parole sentences for juveniles in cases like Graham versus Florida and Miller versus Alabama. [SOURCE: Oregon Supreme Court records] [COMPARE: Kinkle versus person vs Graham versus Florida and Miller versus Alabama]
King argued that his 111year and 8-month sentence functioned as a life sentence for a crime committed when he was 15. [STAT: 111year and 8-month — sentence length] [STAT: 15 — age at crime] [SOURCE: Oregon Supreme Court records]
His argument centered on whether the law should give him a meaningful chance at release given his age and severe mental illness. [SOURCE: legal filing]
In 2018, the Oregon Supreme Court rejected that argument. [DATE: 2018] [SOURCE: Oregon Supreme Court ruling]

=== SCENE 42 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: dense

The court ruled that his sentence was not unconstitutional because the original sentencing judge had considered his youth and psychological condition before imposing it. [SOURCE: Oregon Supreme Court ruling]
The court also focused on the scale of the violence.
Kinko had been sentenced for four murders and 26 attempted murders, which made his case different from the juvenile sentencing cases that involved a single killing or non-homicide crimes. [STAT: four murders — convictions] [STAT: 26 attempted murders — convictions] [SOURCE: Oregon Supreme Court ruling]

=== SCENE 43 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: hold

The most controversial part of the ruling was his treatment of his mental illness.
The court accepted that Kingle had a serious disorder, but emphasized that it was treatable rather than curable and that he could remain dangerous without medication and intensive structure. [SOURCE: Oregon Supreme Court ruling]
In effect, Kingle versus person became a debate over whether a severely mentally ill juvenile offender should ever receive a realistic path to release after committing extreme violence. [SOURCE: legal analysis]

=== SCENE 44 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: hold

Shoplifting. [TEXT: SHOPLIFTING]
Along with some friends, Kip got caught shoplifting CDs from the local Target store. [MAP: local Target store] [SOURCE: police or juvenile records]
Not much else is known about this incident.

=== SCENE 45 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

Keeping houses.
Two weeks before the Thirstston High School shooting, Kinko and several friends toilet papered a house in Springfield, Oregon. [STAT: two weeks before — timeline marker] [MAP: Springfield, Oregon] [SOURCE: later accounts] [TIMELINE: house prank before shooting]
According to later accounts, they used 400 rolls of toilet paper trying to beat a local school record. [STAT: 400 rolls — toilet paper used] [SOURCE: later accounts] [TEXT: 400 ROLLS]

=== SCENE 46 ===
MOOD: tension
PACING: normal

The prank seemed minor at first, but it became important because of what followed.
After Kingo got in trouble, his parents grounded him. [SOURCE: family accounts]
Around this period, his father discovered a locked box containing weapons, including items Kinko had been hiding from his family. [SOURCE: family accounts] [EVIDENCE: locked weapons box]
This entry matters because it shows how close the warning signs were to the surface.
What began as a teenage prank led back to King's hidden weapons only days before the violence at Thirsten High School. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 47 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

Brain abnormalities. [TEXT: BRAIN ABNORMALITIES]
A deeper part of Kinkle's psychological profile came from testimony about possible brain abnormalities. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Neurologist Richard Conor examined Kinkle and said he found several signs of neurological dysfunction. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
These included problems with language, spelling, attention, motor control, sensory processing, and reflexes. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Concord also noted subtle physical asymmetry with the left side of Kinko's body appearing slightly smaller than the right. [SOURCE: expert testimony]

=== SCENE 48 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: dense

In his view, this suggested the possibility of early developmental problems in the brain rather than a recent injury. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Because the left side of the body is controlled by the right side of the brain, some of the findings pointed towards dysfunction in the right hemisphere, a spec scan was also performed. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Concil said that the scan showed decreased activity in several areas, especially the preffrontal, temporal, and partial regions. [SOURCE: spec scan testimony]

=== SCENE 49 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: dense

He described the frontal loes as central to executive function, planning, inhibition, decision-m, and the ability to control behavior over time. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
He also connected the temporal loes to memory, emotion, language, and sensory distortion. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
Congo's overall conclusion was that King had multiple areas of impaired neurological function, especially in regions connected to inhibition, emotional control, and high level thinking. [SOURCE: expert testimony]
He believed these deficits could make Kingle more vulnerable to psychotic episodes, while still saying there was hope for improvement with medication, structure, and proper treatment. [SOURCE: expert testimony]

=== SCENE 50 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

Romeo and Juliet. [TEXT: ROMEO AND JULIET]
King's connection to Romeo and Juliet appeared in several parts of the case. [SOURCE: schoolwork records]
Before leaving for Thirsten High School on the morning of the shooting, he turned the song Leaptood, translated Love Death, from the 1996 film. [DATE: 1996] [SOURCE: police and school records]
The play also appeared in his schoolwork. [SOURCE: schoolwork records]
In one assignment about love at first sight, King wrote that love at first sight existed only in movies where the people in the movies are better than you. [SOURCE: school assignment]
In the same essay, he described love as something that does more harm than good, adding that love was a horrible thing because it could make people kill and hate. [SOURCE: school assignment]

=== SCENE 51 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

King also wrote from the perspective of Tibol, Juliet's violent cousin. [SOURCE: school assignment]
In that assignment, he gave Tibol lines about hatred, anger, and the feeling of killing another person. [SOURCE: school assignment]
The most disturbing line comes after Tibultt takes a life when King has him say that he loved it because it released his anger. [SOURCE: school assignment] [HOLD]

=== SCENE 52 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: hold

This entry is not important because Romeo and Juda caused anything.
It matters because Kinkle seemed drawn to the parts of the story where love, violence, murder, and suicide collapsed into each other.
In his writing, the play became another place where his loneliness and violent fantasies surfaced before the attack. [SOURCE: schoolwork analysis]

=== SCENE 53 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

Prozac. [TEXT: PROZAC]
Before the shooting, King's parents tried to get him help. [SOURCE: therapy records]
They enrolled him in anger management and had him evaluated by psychologist Jeffrey Hicks. [NAME: Jeffrey Hicks — psychologist] [SOURCE: therapy records]
Over several months, Kinkle was prescribed Prozac. [SOURCE: therapy records]
At first, the treatment appeared to help.
After around eight weeks of sessions, Kinkle, his mother, and Hicks agreed that he was doing well enough to stop regular therapy. [STAT: eight weeks — therapy duration] [SOURCE: therapy records]
But when his Prozac prescription ran out in late 1997, Kingle did not refill it. [DATE: late 1997] [SOURCE: medication records]

=== SCENE 54 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: hold

After the attack, his medication history became one of the more debated parts of the case. [SOURCE: case analysis]
Some critics pointed to Prozac or to his decision to stop taking it as a possible factor in his decline. [SOURCE: public commentary]
But reducing the case to medication alone misses the larger picture.
King was dealing with severe psychological distress and a vast array of issues. [SOURCE: psychological records]
This entry matters because Prozac became part of the argument over what warning signs were missed. [SOURCE: case analysis]
It was not a simple explanation for what happened, but it was one piece of a much wider collapse.

=== SCENE 55 ===
MOOD: tension
PACING: normal

Rocks over an overpass. [TEXT: OVERPASS INCIDENT]
King's anger management treatment began after an earlier criminal incident in Bend, Oregon. [MAP: Bend, Oregon] [SOURCE: arrest records]
While attending a snowboarding clinic with a friend, Kinkle was accused of throwing rocks from a highway overpass. [SOURCE: arrest records]
One of the rocks struck a car passing below. [SOURCE: arrest records]
According to accounts of the arrest, police found Kinkle's friend near the overpass and later found Kinkle back at the motel where the boys were staying. [SOURCE: arrest records]

=== SCENE 56 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: normal

When confronted, Kinko reportedly began crying and immediately asked whether anyone had been hurt. [SOURCE: arrest account]
He claimed that his friend had thrown the rock that hit the car, but both boys were charged. [SOURCE: arrest records]
This incident became important because it was one of Kinko's first serious contacts with the justice system. [SOURCE: juvenile records]
Afterward, his parents enrolled him in the anger management that we previously mentioned. [SOURCE: therapy records]

=== SCENE 57 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: normal

Neighbors testimonies. [TEXT: NEIGHBOR TESTIMONIES]
Counts from Kinkle's neighbors show how contradictory his public image was before the shooting. [SOURCE: neighbor interviews]
Some remembered him as quiet and harmless. [SOURCE: neighbor interviews]
Nancy Carvin, whose daughter had played with him for years, described him as a little skinny kid who had always seemed nice. [NAME: Nancy Carvin — neighbor] [SOURCE: neighbor interview]
She said he helped with people's yards and played hide-and-seek around the neighborhood. [SOURCE: neighbor interview]

=== SCENE 58 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

Other neighbors remembered more disturbing behavior. [SOURCE: neighbor interviews]
Betty Anderson said Kingle had once come to her son's 15th birthday party, given him a tool used to break into cars and sprayed the word kill on the driveway with whipped cream. [NAME: Betty Anderson — neighbor] [SOURCE: neighbor interview]
After that, she said he was the one child she told her son not to bring back to the house. [SOURCE: neighbor interview]
This entry matters because it shows how uneven the warning signs looked from the outside.
To some people, Kinkle still appeared like an awkward local kid.
To others, his behavior had already crossed a line.

=== SCENE 59 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: dense

Kinkle versus Lawhead. [TEXT: KINKLE VERSUS LAWHEAD]
Kinkle versus Lawhead was one of Kip Kinkle's postconviction challenges after his original sentence. [SOURCE: postconviction court records]
Unlike Kinkle versus Person, which focused mainly on juvenile sentencing, Lawhead focused on whether his guilty plea should have been accepted in the first place. [COMPARE: Kinkle versus Person vs Lawhead] [SOURCE: postconviction court records]
Kinkle argued that his lawyers had failed him during the plea process. [SOURCE: legal filing]
His attorneys had instructed him to stop taking antiscychotic medication so experts could evaluate his mental condition more accurately. [SOURCE: court records]
Court records later noted that his hallucinations and depression worsened during this period, partly because of the medication withdrawal and the stress of the approaching trial. [SOURCE: court records]

=== SCENE 60 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: dense

Because of this, Kinkle claimed that he had not been competent to plead guilty and that his plea was not truly knowing or voluntary. [SOURCE: legal filing]
He also argued that the court should not have accepted the plea without proper consent from his guardian, Adllet, who had been appointed because King was still a juvenile. [NAME: Adllet — guardian] [SOURCE: court records]
The court rejected the challenge. [SOURCE: court ruling]
They found that despite his mental illness, there was not enough evidence that Kinko had been unable to understand the plea process or assist his lawyers, his attorneys, the trial court, and the mental health experts involved at the time had not treated him as legally incompetent Jonesboro shootings. [SOURCE: court ruling]

=== SCENE 61 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: dense

Just 2 months before thirst, another school shooting occurred on March 24th, 1998. [DATE: March 24th, 1998] [STAT: 2 months before — timeline marker] [SOURCE: newspaper archive] [TIMELINE: Jonesboro before Thirsten]
13-year-old Michelle Johnson and 11-year-old Andrew Golden opened fire at Westside Middle School near Jonesboro, Arkansas, taking the lives of five people and wounding 10 others. [STAT: 13-year-old — shooter age] [NAME: Michelle Johnson — Jonesboro shooter] [STAT: 11-year-old — shooter age] [NAME: Andrew Golden — Jonesboro shooter] [MAP: Westside Middle School near Jonesboro, Arkansas] [STAT: five people — killed] [STAT: 10 others — wounded] [SOURCE: police report]
According to one of King's friends, Kip watched coverage of the Jonesboro shooting and reacted with disturbing interest.
The friend claimed that Kinkle looked at the news and said something like, "Hey, that's pretty cool." Journal. [QUOTE: Hey, that's pretty cool.] [SOURCE: friend account] [TEXT: THAT’S PRETTY COOL]

=== SCENE 62 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: hold

Writings from Kinkle's journal share insight on his mindset before and after killing his parents before the murders. [SOURCE: journal archive] [EVIDENCE: journal] [CHAPTER: Mind In Collapse]
His writing is dominated by loneliness and his growing self-hatred. [SOURCE: journal archive]
In one entry, he writes, "I sit here all alone. [QUOTE: I sit here all alone.] [SOURCE: journal]
I am always alone." The repetition makes the feeling seem inescapable, as if isolation has become the center of his entire identity. [QUOTE: I am always alone.] [SOURCE: journal]
He also frames his suffering in almost religious terms, crying out to God as though his problems have moved beyond ordinary human help.
He writes, "Oh God, I am so close to killing people." before asking why God wanted him to live in complete misery. [QUOTE: Oh God, I am so close to killing people.] [SOURCE: journal]

=== SCENE 63 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: hold

The entry then turns back to weapons.
After mentioning that his parents were trying to take away some of his guns, Kinko writes that his guns are the only thing that haven't stabbed me in the back. [SOURCE: journal] [EVIDENCE: guns]
That line is one of the most revealing in the journal. [SOURCE: journal analysis]
It shows how much his thinking had warped.
People had become sources of rejection and betrayal while weapons have become the only things he felt he could trust. [SOURCE: journal analysis]
He writes that his eyes hurt and feel like they're trying to crawl out of his head. [SOURCE: journal]
And at this point, the entry reads like a mind collapsing under paranoia and despair. [SOURCE: journal analysis]

=== SCENE 64 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: hold

After killing his parents, Kingle's writing shifts from resentment into panic and guilt. [SOURCE: journal archive]
He opens by admitting what he has done, then immediately collapses into confusion.
Quote, "I have just killed my parents. [QUOTE: I have just killed my parents.] [SOURCE: journal] [TEXT: KILLED MY PARENTS] [SILENCE] [HOLD]
I don't know what is happening." The entry is disturbing because he does not write about them with hatred. [QUOTE: I don't know what is happening.] [SOURCE: journal]
He says he loves them, calling them wonderful people and describes himself as a horrible son who did not deserve them. [SOURCE: journal]

=== SCENE 65 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: hold

His explanation is fractured.
Part of him seems to believe he killed them because the shame of his arrest would have destroyed them. [SOURCE: journal analysis]
He writes that his parents could not handle the embarrassment of his two felony charges, but the rest of the entry points to something much deeper than shame. [SOURCE: journal]
He says that his head just doesn't work right. [SOURCE: journal]
Curses the voice inside his head and repeats that he wants his life to end. [SOURCE: journal]
The most chilling contradiction comes when he writes, "I want to be gone, but I have to kill people. [QUOTE: I want to be gone, but I have to kill people.] [SOURCE: journal] [HOLD]
I don't know why." That line captures the collapse of King ruined by guilt and violent compulsion all existing at once. [QUOTE: I don't know why.] [SOURCE: journal]

=== SCENE 66 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: hold

He apologizes repeatedly but still ends with the same fatal logic that he mentioned in the walkthrough. [SOURCE: journal analysis]
I have no other choice. [QUOTE: I have no other choice.] [SOURCE: journal] [TEXT: NO OTHER CHOICE]
This journal entry is important because it shows King immediately after the first murders before the school shooting had even happened. [SOURCE: journal archive]
He's not presenting a clear ideology or a coherent motive.
Unlike most other mass shooters who write something before they commit their attack, he's presenting his mind in crisis. [COMPARE: coherent manifesto vs mind in crisis] [SOURCE: case analysis]
Aware that he has destroyed his family but still convinced that more violence is somehow inevitable. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 67 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

How to join Church of Satan. [TEXT: CHURCH OF SATAN]
One obscure detail from King's school life involves a speech class assignment. [SOURCE: student accounts]
Kinko reportedly gave a how-to speech about making a bomb, which should have been an obvious warning sign. [SOURCE: student accounts]
But according to students in the class, the presentation did not stand out as much as it should have, partly because another student had given a speech on how to join the Church of Satan Disneyland. [SOURCE: student accounts]

=== SCENE 68 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

One of the stranger details from King's psychological evaluations was his fixation on Disney. [TEXT: DISNEY FIXATION] [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
Dr.
Orin Bolstad said that when he asked Kinko about the Disney corporation, Kinkle became unusually animated. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
Up to that point, his effect had been mostly flat, but the subject of Disney seemed to trigger a much more intense response. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]

=== SCENE 69 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: normal

Kinko claimed that the Disney dollar would eventually take over the American dollar, and that Disney was gaining control over news and entertainment. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
He connected the company to censorship, corporate power, and government control while describing Mickey Mouse as a harmless mascot that allowed Disney to avoid criticism. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
when he repeated the phrase Disneyland, the happiest place on earth. [QUOTE: Disneyland, the happiest place on earth.] [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
Bolstad said he did it in a cynical and agitated way. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]

=== SCENE 70 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

The Disney fixation also blended into wider paranoid ideas. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
Kinkle talked about censorship, dictators, government experiments, and microchips being placed in people. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
He even said that when he had an MRI, he wondered whether doctors would find a chip in his head. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]
From there, he connected the idea of a chip to the voices he was hearing, suggesting that maybe this was how he was being controlled. [SOURCE: psychological evaluation]

=== SCENE 71 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: normal

Letters from prison. [TEXT: LETTERS FROM PRISON]
In 2017, Kinkle replied to a 16-year-old girl who had written to him while struggling with her own mental health. [DATE: 2017] [STAT: 16-year-old — letter writer age] [SOURCE: prison correspondence]
The letter is significant because of his tone.
Kinkle does not encourage her fixation on him or romanticize what happened. [SOURCE: prison correspondence]
Instead, he tells her to ask for an adult for help, especially if she's hearing voices or feeling trapped inside her own thoughts. [SOURCE: prison correspondence]
He also writes that he would normally not respond to a 16-year-old, but that her letter worried him enough to make an exception. [STAT: 16-year-old — age mentioned] [SOURCE: prison correspondence]
He urges her to take what he says seriously, to trust someone close to her, and to hold on because things can get better. [SOURCE: prison correspondence]

=== SCENE 72 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: hold

This entry matters because it shows King decades after the attack, looking back on his own childhood through the lens of regret. [SOURCE: prison correspondence analysis]
The letter does not undo what he did, but it shows how his case continued to intersect with questions of mental illness and the possibility of someone warning another person away from the same path. [SOURCE: prison correspondence analysis]

=== SCENE 73 ===
MOOD: tragic
PACING: normal

His true motive, one of the clearest motives running through the Kingill case was suicide. [TEXT: TRUE MOTIVE]
He did not appear to imagine the attack ending with escape or survival. [SOURCE: case analysis]
During the shooting, after students tackled and restrained him, Kingle shouted for them to kill him. [SOURCE: witness statements]
The same pattern continued after his arrest.
During questioning, Kingle lunged the detective Al Wan with a knife he had hidden on his body. [NAME: Al Wan — detective] [SOURCE: police report] [EVIDENCE: hidden knife]
This has often been read as another suic act, not an attempt to escape, but an attempt to force police into killing him. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 74 ===
MOOD: dark
PACING: hold

His journal points in the same direction.
After murdering his parents, Kinkle repeatedly wrote that he wanted to die and wanted to be gone while also claiming he still to kill people. [SOURCE: journal archive]
That contradiction is central to the attack.
It was not driven by one clean motive, but suicide with one of his strongest forces, Alvaro Castillo. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 75 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

One connection sometimes noted between Kipkle and Alvaro Castillo is the day of Castello's attack. [NAME: Alvaro Castillo — later school shooter] [SOURCE: case comparison]
On August 30th, 2006, 18-year-old Alvaro Raphael Castillo killed his father at home before opening fire at Orange High School in North Carolina. [DATE: August 30th, 2006] [STAT: 18-year-old — attacker age] [MAP: Orange High School in North Carolina] [SOURCE: police report]
August 30th is also Kip King's birthday. [DATE: August 30th] [SOURCE: biographical records]
Castillo was known to be heavily fixated on Coline, but there is no clear evidence that the date was chosen because of King. [SOURCE: case analysis]
For that reason, it should not be presented as part of a direct copycat chain.
At most, it remains an eerie coincidence.

=== SCENE 76 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

The Becoming by 9in Nails. [TEXT: THE BECOMING]
One small detail connected to Kinko's writings is a possible link to 9-in nails song the becoming from the downward spiral. [SOURCE: music and case commentary]
After the case, some noticed that one of Kinko's lines about the voices in his head sounded similar to a lyric from the song this voice inside my head. [QUOTE: this voice inside my head] [SOURCE: music and case commentary]
This does not mean the music caused the attack. [SOURCE: case analysis]
King may have latched on to music that reflected feelings he already had.
Depression, alienation, self-hatred, and a fractured sense of identity. [SOURCE: case analysis]
The downward spiral is filled with imagery of psychological collapse. [SOURCE: music archive]
So it is not surprising that someone in kinkle state might have found parts of it relatable.

=== SCENE 77 ===
MOOD: neutral
PACING: hold

This matters because it shows how easily cultural material can become part of the debate after a tragedy.
Some people look for blame in media, but the more useful question is why certain lyrics resonated with him in the first place.
In King's case, the connection seems less like a cause and more like a reflection of the darkness already present in his own writing. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 78 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

Possible cause of Coline. [TEXT: POSSIBLE CAUSE OF COLINE] [CHAPTER: Echoes After Thirsten]
One of the more speculative entries in this case is Kip's possible connection to the imagery that later became associated with Coline. [SOURCE: case comparison]
Kingo wore a trench coat during the Thirstston attack because it allowed him to conceal the weapons he was carrying. [SOURCE: police report] [EVIDENCE: trench coat]
Less than a year later, the Coline perpetrators would become heavily associated with trench coats. [SOURCE: newspaper archive] [COMPARE: Thirstston trench coat vs Coline trench coats]
Even though there is no solid evidence that thisa specific detail came from King, there were other overlaps, too.
Kingle had an interest in explosives, used bomb-making materials such as the anarchist cookbook, and reportedly listened to Marilyn Manson. [SOURCE: case comparison] [EVIDENCE: anarchist cookbook] [EVIDENCE: Marilyn Manson references]

=== SCENE 79 ===
MOOD: curious
PACING: normal

All of which became later part of the cultural discussion around Coline. [SOURCE: media archive]
But these similarities need to be handled carefully.
They may reflect the same late 1990s atmosphere, a violent media panic, alienated teenage identity, and fascination with mass destruction rather than direct influence. [SOURCE: cultural analysis]
This becomes interesting when placed beside Eric Harris's own insistence that Coline was not meant to be a copycat attack. [SOURCE: writings archive]
In his writings, Harris directly said that they were not trying to copy anyone. [SOURCE: writings archive]
But that claim was never entirely convincing because Coline itself was built from a wider culture of influence.
The Oklahoma City bombing, violent media, doom, military imagery, and more. [SOURCE: cultural analysis]

=== SCENE 80 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: hold

The point is not that Kip caused it, but that his case came first and already contained several elements that would later become familiar in the post Coline profile. [SOURCE: case comparison]
Hidden weapons, trench co imagery, explosives, violent writings, and a desire to create catastrophic harm. [SOURCE: case comparison] [TEXT: HIDDEN WEAPONS]
After Coline happened, King was worried that he may have contributed to it. [SOURCE: case analysis]
Whether that fear was justified is impossible to prove, but it shows how thirsten sound easily at the edge for changing era. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 81 ===
MOOD: mystery
PACING: normal

Pekka Eric Alvin connection. [TEXT: PEKKA CONNECTION]
One of the most obscure later connections to the Kinko case comes from the Joker school shooting in Finland. [NAME: Pekka Eric Alvin — Joker school shooter] [MAP: Finland] [SOURCE: documentary archive]
After Pekka Eric Alvin carried out the attack in 2007, one of his online friends was left questioning whether she could have done anything to stop him. [DATE: 2007] [SOURCE: documentary archive]
In a documentary, she explains that she thought of Kipkinkle because he had also attacked his school. [SOURCE: documentary archive]
But unlike many perpetrators, he had survived and was still in prison.
She decided to write to him hoping he might understand whether anyone could have recognized the warning signs or prevented what happened. [SOURCE: documentary archive]

=== SCENE 82 ===
MOOD: emotional
PACING: hold

King apparently wrote back. [SOURCE: prison correspondence]
His answer was blunt.
There was nothing his own friends could have done and he believed there was nothing she could have done to stop Pekky either. [SOURCE: prison correspondence] [SILENCE] [HOLD]
This entry is important because it turns Kinko into strange point of reference long after Thirsten.
His case was not just remembered as a pre- Cololine shooting.
It became something later people connected to similar tragedies and looked back to when trying to understand guilt warning signs and whether intervention had ever truly been possible. [SOURCE: case analysis]

=== SCENE 83 ===
MOOD: calm
PACING: hold

And with that, the iceberg is finished. [TEXT: ICEBERG FINISHED]
Thank you for watching until the very end.
I really appreciate it.
Make sure to subscribe and follow my Twitter if you enjoyed, and I'll see you in the next video.
